speaker: interviewer
text: Martin Koby, Tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's still ticking away. Still the tension is there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed in that pit, like I told you, part of December, the whole month of January. The end of January, we began to hear artillery. Out in the country, you can hear artillery fire a long-- far away-- and missed the shots. And they used to give us-- they used to come up to the pigsty, light a cigarette, and used to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father used to get out, you know, to this-- you know, it's like a hole, like in a tank, you know, armour tank. Only one person can fit in-- and missed the shots. And they used to give us reports, you know? The Russians are already taken Kiev. And they've taken this village and this city. And they're on the march. And they're already in Hungary. What's the matter with them? We are more important than Hungary. Why they are-- why? Because they were-- they were like a circle, you know? They went on the edges. Because the Germans were holding the front line.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, Mr. [? Scastny ?] remember, the Russians were in our village. They where in our part of the country for about, like, what, two years almost, June of-- no, September of '39 till June of '41, when the Germans attacked them. And they used to have all kinds of courses-- sub defense, you know, and all kinds of classes. They taught about army and the movements and stuff, something like militia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, everybody-- well, I used to go. Why not? They didn't chase me out. And they used to show all kinds of things, how army works, how to defend yourself, where to hide, how camouflage. If we got a kid, big deal. Well, I picked up a few things.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Scastny ?] comes over. The [? master ?] news is that the Ru-- the Germans are abandoning Rovno. They're, you know, they're running. Because the Russians might-- they are afraid they're going to surround them. But they already took the city of Klevan. Klevan-- that's where my cousin, Isak Uster lived with his mother, Malka [INAUDIBLE]-- used to live. And we couldn't understand. Everything is going around the edges except our part. Well, he comes back. And he says, it's true. I don't know what's going to happen. But it's going to be a problems. The Germans are putting up artillery on a, a hill just east of us, along-- parallel to the highway, maybe 100 yards. And it goes like an elevation. So from the village, you cannot see the highway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-oh. And he says that the-- [? Scastny ?] said, think the Russians are going to attack us from-- you know-- going to be close to our village from the-- from Klevan and Lutsk over there from the, from the west, which was 20 kilometers, you know? So I figured out that the Germans are going to schlob artillery shells across our barn, across our village, because they're coming from the west, the Russians. Well, they shouldn't. Let me tell you, that was a hell of a night. Oh, god. You could see-- hear the Russians attacking. And you know, the Russians, when they attack, you know, with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] foot soldiers. What they call foot soldiers? Infantry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Infantry when the infantry attacks, they holler hurrah. And you can hear hurrah and hurrah, you know, all this screaming, sometimes louder and sometimes softer. Because must have been a [? wipe. ?] And these artillery shells are lobbing, not in our village. They must-- but you know, when the artillery shell flies, it makes all this racket. God. You sit there in that pit. And you don't know. You say, if one falls in here, good bye, Charlie-- all gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, somehow, we survived the night. In the morning, we knew that the Germans removed their artillery from the field. And they went west to Dub-- to the city of Dubno. Mr. [? Scastny ?] came and told us all about that. And we were saying, we're going to sit and wait what's going to happen. He says, you know, the Russians are here. The Russians are there. Who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10 o'clock, 10:30, my father got into the, in that turret, the hole. And he looked out. He said, oh, there is a Russian soldier going, you know, the side street over there. Oh, there's a Russian coming here on a, on a, on a, on a, on a pony. He said, the Russians have such short horses. Well, these were Mongolian ponies. Because the Germans, they did-- in Western Russia, they had all these nice horses. But they got destroyed during the, the war. Or the Germans took them all back to Germany. Under the Germans, they used to take everything from Russia, whatever they could. If it walked, it walked to Germany. There were one or two Germans. And they had whoever recruited [INAUDIBLE]. And they used to-- you know, sheep and goats and horses and cows. They couldn't go out the main road, because they-- so they walked them through the villages. Because there would be fodder and water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians, when they [INAUDIBLE]-- when they coming back west, you know, I got smart about this later on. After the war, I figured it out. And this Russian soldier comes over on this little pony, you know, with this big overcoat, long robe, and his feet almost dragging on the ground. And the horses, you know, had this like a mane on their legs, you know, hairy hind-- hairy legs on the bottom. Later, I learned those were Mongolian ponies. They're short and [INAUDIBLE] comes in into the bac-- into the yard. And he stands in the middle of the yard. And Mr. [? Scastny ?] comes over to him. And he says, how do you do? And they talk about it. And the Russian says to him-- my father is up. And I'm up. I almost choked because my father was a big man, but I could see him. He had a wart on his cheek, I remember, big, red wart. And he says to him, Mr. [? Scastny ?] you have any Germans in your barns and stuff? He says, no I don't-- no, no German in my barns. Good. He says, you have any tobacco? He says, yeah, I got tobacco. He said, can I have a smoke? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. [? Scastny ?] used to have a pouch, the bladder from a pig. That's what used to be the-- kept the-- you know, they were-- whatever-- treated or made sanitary. They were dry. And he had this tobacco pouch. And the, the in was in this urinary-- ureter, you know, bag, pouch. And the Russian takes out a piece of newspaper. And the cigarette must have been long as a cigar or something. I don't know. And here, Mr. [? Scastny ?] pours them in, you know, this tobacco. It's toba-- was tobacco and the stems mixture. Because you got to economize the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And he lights up. And he-- must have given him a lot of pleasure. And he says to Mr. [? Scastny ?] so how about one more for that road? Mr. [? Scastny ?] says, no. He didn't give it to him. I felt bad. You know, I thought-- I said, gee. Who knows he will survive, give it to him. No, he wouldn't give it to him. The guy went away. And the next morning, we got out. We went home to our house. But one side story, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] remember when I was telling you that, you know, they were lobbing shells? This soldier on the horse came the second day. Because the first day after the-- there was still going on-- the battle was still going on. You know, there were, like clean-up operations. Two soldiers come to Mr. [? Scastny's ?] Because in the back of us-- back of the pigsty where we were, the west part, was a big field, a big open field. So I guess they wandered into there. And they came to Mr. [? Scastny's ?] house. And they asked his permission-- and all the men, a mature man and a young, young man, oh, maybe, who knows, 18, 19, a young kid. And is it OK to come in in the house? They have to have to something-- their machine gun, you know, with a disk on top of it. I think they call them [RUSSIAN SPEECH]-- a Russian gun with a semi-automatic or an automatic, like a machine gun, but with a disk ON IT.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says to Mr. [? Scastny, ?] we want to come and warm up our hands, because we have to fix this, this machine, this, this gun. So he says, sure. He's not going to say no to him. And Mr. [? Scastny ?] was telling us later on. They were sitting. So he asked this-- all the men, these two men, he wanted to know. There were rumors under the German that Stalin died. You know, they said this war could not go on without Stalin. But that's-- you know, this is afterthought, so it's very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the man tells-- told Mr. [? Scastny ?] oh, the devil knows. You know, it's an expression, a Russian expression. The devil knows. Who knows. So what do you mean? He says, I don't know. I only know that I have to take care of this machine gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were sitting there for a while. Finally, a third soldier comes around. And he's hollering, calling this man's name. And he's hollering, and cussing, and swearing. And here I am, standing with-- you know, my mother is here, and all this cussing going on. He goes in the house. And they come out. And they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. [? Scastny ?] told me the man said to him, you know, I know how to take this machine gun apart. But how do you put it together? Couldn't put it together. So they put it back. And they went away, fighting. This was part of the team that hollered hurrah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man-- now, the man with the wart, the soldier, he came today-- tomorrow morning, the change of whether, snow, cold, terrible. We got out of the hole at about 4 o'clock in the morning. It just-- daybreak in the winter. We went into the back-- the back of the, of the pigsty, a [? pig of ?] the barn. And we went home. We could have gone through the front. But then we would have known-- people would have noticed, whoever was awake, that we were-- came from Mr. [? Scastny's ?] This way, no one knew that we were-- had anything to do with Mr. [? Scastny. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Came home-- guess what? The house is full of Russian soldiers. They're sleeping on the floor, on the table, under the table. There was one guy sleeping on three chairs. My parent's chairs were still standing there, because people lived in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the officer wanted to move. And he was sleeping on the chair, and on three chairs. He was kind of dark complected, you know, some was from the [? Caucasus. ?] He was not a, a Russian by itself. He was one of the ethnic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's got this Tommy gun, you know, on his chest. And he's hugging it, you know. And he says you know-- whatever he-- forget what he-- I don't remember what he called him. And I was watching another soldier with a Tommy gun. Wow, big guy, right? And he says to him, you know, I want you to move. Because you're in the way. We can't go by. [INAUDIBLE] And he keeps talking to him, talking. He says, I know how to get him up. He hollers at him, whatever his name, sir, we are surrounded. Guess what? Got up just like that. Then he moved to another place, of you know, seat. And we bought with us-- brought with us from Mr. [? Scastny's ?] home, the clothes were scattered. We knew we were full of lice there, but that's beside the point. Because lice, no one sees, right? But you see all this-- the rags, you know, because you lie on straw. Blankets, forget it. They were all gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: They threw the strips of cloth-- you know, holes all over the place. God, terrible. And my parents went away somewhere. They told us [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], myself and my brother. We stayed in the house. And the people that, you know, took over the house, [? stayed there third. ?] No one said-- we had no discussion. We were told not to argue with them, not to discuss anything, sit quietly and talk to the soldiers. Don't talk to the natives. And they went away somewheres. And about an hour, two hours later, they came back. They brought some potatoes, a couple loaves of bread, some flour, vegetable oil. I don't know where they got it or from who. I have no idea. And they said, pick up. We're going to Rovno. Now, this is February. And it's winter. And it's cold. And it's snow. You can't go-- we have-- we couldn't survive to go by foot, on foot. We got up to the-- we walked all the way to the highway. And we hitchhiked. And we went to Rovno. That was our liberation day. And so you have to find a house somewhere. [? So you know, ?] you know, you have to-- first, you have to scout. So we ended up on Dubin-- [INAUDIBLE] Street, which went from Rovno to Dubno to Lviv. Wasn't, I don't know, maybe 30 kilometers or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we got into by the cemetery. There was this main street was called [? Dubinska. ?] We found a house which was abandoned. There were no doors. There were no windows. There were no stove. And if you had a stove, big deal. What are you going to burn in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I noticed that the neighborhood houses, which used to be-- don't forget-- a, mainly a Jewish city. All-- a lot of the houses were missing the doors and the windows and part of the walls. People used to cannibalize. You needed wood, you want it, tore out the house, and you burn it. Well, we were not ready yet for that. They went to the city. And they found a place. While they were in the city, you know, you see all these troop movement-- trucks and soldiers, infantry walking, you know, soldiers on skis-- you know, walking the ski-- dog teams, you know, pulling sleds. There is tremendous movements like, you know, like Georgia. Georgia is on fire, but this is in the winter, you know, troop movement, you know-- And see all this-- people running back and forth. This is soldiers. And I'm standing there on the street and admiring all this movement. And-- but I was told to guard the house. Because we got all this food there. Sure, I will. And I see this across the street, a big banner. And you know, I walked a little bit. And I looked-- Stalin's picture. Ah, does you good. Just because you find out late-- years later what a rotten man he was, that's beside the point. But Stalin liberated you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm standing there. And I'm watching this parade. And see down-- I wanted to go and see that picture of Stalin, definitely. And you go-- and I see all these people, a bunch of people, maybe a dozen and a half, walking with two soldiers with their guns. And they have the knives on there. What's it called? You know, every soldier has a-- Bayonet.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bayonets stuck on there. That means it's ready to go. And I was watching that and said, oh, poor suckers got arrested for something, huh? No. He comes up to me. And I'm standing there. But first, I want to tell you, we were all skin and bone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do I know? Because when we got out to in our house, I looked at my hands. I remember that. And I put my hands and fingers-- you know, my fingers like this together. And there was nothing but valleys in between. It was just-- you could see the bones, actually. You know, we have these indentations here in the knuckles? You could see the little indentations on each side. You could just real-- You can't see now through. You could not-- you could see right through the bones, you know, right between the finger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yo know, I couldn't hit my foot. My brother lost his voice. He could not speak before. He always spoke softly. And he-- you know, he was a young kid, so lost his poke, but everything soft. He could not pronounce loud.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm standing there, this big hero with full of lice. And he come-- the two soldiers and the other come up. He says, how do you do? [INAUDIBLE] How do you do? Greetings. Where do you live? This house. Good. Do you have a pick and shovel? I said, no. What do you mean you don't have pick and shovel? Don't know. Don't have-- we don't have one. Good. You come with us. Said, what do you mean? We're going to go and, you know, dig over there the defense ditches, anti-tank ditches.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I said, comrade, I say, I can't. You know, I'm in a poor shape. You just liberated us today.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, that's good. Now you come along with us. And we're going to liberate the others. [LAUGHS] You know, we liberated you. Now you come and help us liberate the others. So what am I going to do? I, you know, I start saying, I can't go. My father and mother-- so he starts clicking with the gun. So I said, yes, sir. We go right away. We went with him. I almost died of frostbite over there. I almost-- you know how cold it was? Ah, terrible. You see the airplanes flying back and forth. They didn't drop any bombs, the Russian airplanes. And you can hear artillery gunfire far away somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to the field. And it's drafty. And it's cold. And it's fro-- the, the soil is frozen. They put me some-- with some people there and gave me a, a pick and a shovel. I couldn't even lift the pick, leave along the shovel. Well, I stayed there for about three or four hours. Finally, one guy came around. And he says, get out of here. Go home. I would have been better off staying there because I would have marched back with some people. And here, I have to march on my own. I finally got home. Oh, boy. Everything broke loose, you know, because my uncles were there. One of my-- you know my uncles were there and some of the other-- the [? Yedlins ?] were there. And they told me, you know, how responsible. I am. You know, I left the house. I was told to be in charge. And here you were responsible teenager-- screaming and all. They won't listen to my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what am I going to do? I'm going to fire-- suffer the indignities of my, my parents and my relatives and with friends. Years later-- they found a house. We moved into the city. And years-- not a year-- a year later-- this was '44. And the summer of '45, they used to have volunteer projects. The Russians, the communists, had volunteers. You devoted a day to, to, to the, to the good of society. Used to call it a sabbatical, not a sabbatical in our sense, [POLISH SPEECH], that means Saturday you gave to the government. Saturday, you Jews give the day to God. Here, we're going to give to society. What was the project? We have to go and cover up those-- you know, straighten out the fields. Because they dug those anti-tank ditches. You've got to cover them up. It would be a waste of, of, of land. So I went with my father. My father was volunteer. And he says, you want to come along? I said, yes. I've got to go with my father, right, just me and him. Well, we got there. And we're shoveling the dirt back in. It's in the summer, and-- I said, Pa you remember when we came to Rovno in the winter [INAUDIBLE], you know, I was disappeared? This is where I was, digging these ditches right over here. He said, you're kidding? I said, no, I'm not kidding. This is what-- this is where I was that day. So he said, I'm sorry we hollered at you. [LAUGHS] What other towns did you visit on your way to America after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, we were-- since we were Polish citizens, the Russians, you know, they had a political deal with the West or something. And they told us we can move to Poland. So people thought, you know, it's going to be like Poland before the war. It's not a-- it's not going to be communist. As much as people accuse the Jews of being communists, 95-- 90% of the Jews did not subscribe to communist ideolog-- the idea-- now, you know the id-- of membership or something like that. From what I understand, it was a social and economic subscription. First of all, at least you were not persecuted. Everybod-- Russian propaganda was or the idea-- ideology was, everybody is equal. There is no ethnic discrimination. Your-- everybody has equal rights. That's what Jews were looking for. They were not looking for making everybody equal and happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they thought that Poland after the war would be like Poland before the war, which would be much nicer than being under communist. No self expression. No economic opportunities. No regimentation. And from Poland, it was easier to leave Poland, go some place else other than Russia. So everybody jumped on the wagon, we went to Poland, came to Poland. Guess what? The communist sent power, which is we ended up in a city called Bytom, Upper, Silesia. That's the Polish name not far from Katowice because you could take the street car from Bytom too. Nice town. Very nice city. Always liked it. And the German name for it was Beuthen. I think it was not far on the Pole-- Germ-- Pole-- the border between Germany and Poland. Because there was a big population-- big German population. And the whole thing was organized in a German style, you know, paved streets, big apartments, ma-- controlled the amount of electricity that you could use. We found an apartment there. And if you have-- we did know about it. You turn on the kitchen light, it was fine. You turn on the living room light, fine. You turn on the bedroom light, racket, noisy. Because you know, they had an alarm built in. If you use more three bu-- more than two bulbs, like the amount of wattage, that set off the alarm. You have to turn off the light. But who knew? We finally found out how it was. So we stayed in Bytom, for about a year. And then there Haganah-- Mr. Ben-Gurion's people organized exodus-- underground exodus from Poland. You could leave legally. But there was all kinds of movement. You know, people were leaving from Germany to go to Russia, Greeks, you know, to Greece, and all kinds of people movement. Because the Germans included a lot of slave labor to work for them. So the people from the Jewish resistance and the Haganah moved in, you know, to immigration. But it was not easy. Somehow, my parents found, found a way out with a, a religious group. But you know, you have to pay everybody. You have to pay everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want to cross the border illegally, you got to pay. You want to take the train without a, you know, you had to have a permission to travel. You got to pay. So all of this had to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, he came. And he says, tonight, we'll leave. You take with you what you can carry. Ah! We carried pillow ca-- pillows and blankets and pots and pans. You know, what are you going to do with them? You don't know where you're going. You know, If you don't have no experience, you're being a genuine refugee, or moving fast, you take all kinds of garbage that you have to throw out. And there, we crossed the border. We went on a train. Before we entered Czechoslovakia, the train stopped. We got robbed. You know, they went through our things. They searched us, whatever, you know? They were looking for-- they knew that everybody who is moving, you know, got to have money. Or if they have-- if they don't have money, they have something else. They're going to take it away. Well, some people got caught with a [INAUDIBLE], you know, got robbed by those that were letting you go, you know, that was already paid off. So they did that a second time. What are you going to do, scream and holler? So we got back on the train. We came to Prague-- big, big railroad station, you know, with big roof, oh, just-- you wondered how it's standing up like that. You know, you didn't see things like that before.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in a camp outside of Prague. We had a lot of fleas also. But we stayed about two weeks the most. And then we went to the American zone. That was the main destination, the nice Americans.
